pg409-p1-s1:
tion: At the end of study time, I would drink two
beers, jump in bed, sleep real fast, and wake up smart.
pg409-p1:
tion: At the end of study time, I would drink two
beers, jump in bed, sleep real fast, and wake up smart.
pg409-p2-s1:
I drank my way through schools and always got
honors.
pg409-p2-s2:
And as I went through pharmacy school,
graduate school, medical school, internship, residency,
and specialty training, and finally, went into practice,
my drinking kept increasing.
pg409-p2-s3:
But I thought it was because
my responsibilities were increasing. “If you had
my responsibilities, if you needed the sleep like I do,
you’d drink too.”
pg409-p2:
I drank my way through schools and always got
honors. And as I went through pharmacy school,
graduate school, medical school, internship, residency,
and specialty training, and finally, went into practice,
my drinking kept increasing. But I thought it was because
my responsibilities were increasing. “If you had
my responsibilities, if you needed the sleep like I do,
you’d drink too.”
pg409-p3-s1: My drinking took place after work hours.
pg409-p3-s2:
I remember
finding myself in the middle of the night in the
doctors’ parking lot at the hospital with one foot in
the car and one foot on the ground, not knowing
which was the lead foot; finding myself hanging up the
telephone—then realizing I had gotten out of bed, answered
the phone, turned on the light, and carried on
a conversation with a patient.
pg409-p3-s3:
I didn’t know whether I
had told him to rush to the hospital and I’d meet him
there, or to take two aspirin and call me in the morning.
pg409-p3-s4:
With a problem like that, I couldn’t go back to
sleep.
pg409-p3-s5:
So I’d sit up, watch old Wallace Beery movies
on all-night TV, and drink.
pg409-p3:
My drinking took place after work hours. I remember
finding myself in the middle of the night in the
doctors’ parking lot at the hospital with one foot in
the car and one foot on the ground, not knowing
which was the lead foot; finding myself hanging up the
telephone—then realizing I had gotten out of bed, answered
the phone, turned on the light, and carried on
a conversation with a patient. I didn’t know whether I
had told him to rush to the hospital and I’d meet him
there, or to take two aspirin and call me in the morning. With a problem like that, I couldn’t go back to
sleep. So I’d sit up, watch old Wallace Beery movies
on all-night TV, and drink.
pg409-p4-s1:
The longer the drinking continued, the shorter the
time the alcohol would keep me asleep; I would have
to drink myself back to sleep again and again throughout
the night.
pg409-p4-s2: But I never became a morning drinker.
pg409-p4-s3: Instead, I had a 5:00 a.m. shutoff time.
pg409-p4-s4:
If it was one
minute before five, I’d drink myself back to sleep.
pg409-p4-s5:
If it
was one minute after, I’d stay up and act like a martyr
all day.
pg409-p4-s6:
It became progressively harder to get up in
the morning, until one day I asked myself what I
would do for a patient who felt this rotten.
pg409-p4:
The longer the drinking continued, the shorter the
time the alcohol would keep me asleep; I would have
to drink myself back to sleep again and again throughout
the night. But I never became a morning drinker. Instead, I had a 5:00 a.m. shutoff time. If it was one
minute before five, I’d drink myself back to sleep. If it
was one minute after, I’d stay up and act like a martyr
all day. It became progressively harder to get up in
the morning, until one day I asked myself what I
